name: Uttarakhand Tejpatta
description: Organic aromatic bay leaves (Cinnamomum tamala) grown in the high-altitude regions of Chamoli district, Garhwal Himalayas. Known for their unique culinary aroma and medicinal properties.
ingredients: Pahadi Rajma: Munsyari Rajma, Tejpatta, ghee, onions, tomatoes, ginger-garlic paste, spices
Basmati Pulao: Basmati Rice, Tejpatta, cumin seeds, whole spices, ghee, water
Traditional Garhwali Curry (Chainsoo): Black gram, ghee, Tejpatta, garlic, coriander, spices
Tejpatta Herbal Tea: Water, Tejpatta (dry leaves), ginger, honey
recipe: Pahadi Rajma: Boil Rajma. Saute onions, ginger-garlic paste, tomatoes, and Tejpatta in ghee. Add spices, mix boiled Rajma, simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Basmati Pulao: Fry Tejpatta, cumin, cloves in ghee. Add soaked Basmati rice and water. Cover and cook until dry.
Traditional Garhwali Curry: Roast ground black gram in ghee. Temper with garlic, Tejpatta, and spices. Add water and simmer until cooked.
Tejpatta Herbal Tea: Boil water with Tejpatta and crushed ginger for 5 minutes. Strain and sweeten with honey.
recipe_description: Tejpatta is an aromatic bay leaf used as a flavoring ingredient in many dishes. It cannot be eaten directly but infuses flavor during cooking.

name: Basmati Rice
description: Premium aromatic long-grain rice variety with a distinctive fragrance, soft texture, and exceptional cooking quality, cultivated in the fertile Himalayan sub-tracts.
recipe: Wash and soak the Basmati Rice for 20-30 minutes.
Heat ghee in a pot and add Tejpatta, cloves, and cinnamon stick until aromatic.
Add the soaked rice and saute gently for 2 minutes.
Add water and salt, bring to a boil, then cover and simmer on low heat for 12-15 minutes until water is absorbed.
recipe_description: Fragrant and aromatic long-grain rice dish cooked with local whole spices and ghee.

name: Aipan Art
description: Traditional ritualistic folk art painted by Kumaoni women, utilizing white rice paste (biswar) over a red ochre (geru) background to create geometric and symbolic patterns.
uses: Ritual floor drawings for temples and entrances
Wall decorations during festivals and weddings

name: Munsyari Rajma
description: Premium high-altitude white kidney beans grown organically in the pristine Munsyari valley. Famous for their rich taste, high protein content, and soft cooking texture.
uses: Preparation of traditional Himalayan Rajma curry
ingredients: 1 Tejpatta, cumin seeds, coriander powder, turmeric, red chili, salt
recipe: Pressure cook the soaked Rajma with water, salt, and Tejpatta for 5-6 whistles until soft.
Heat oil or ghee in a pan, temper with cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Saute chopped onions until golden brown, then add ginger-garlic paste and pureed tomatoes.
Add spices (turmeric, coriander, chili powder) and cook until the oil separates.
Pour in the boiled Rajma along with its water, mix well, and simmer on medium heat for 15 minutes.
recipe_description: A signature kidney bean curry from the high-altitude valley of Munsyari, cooked with local herbs.

name: Ringaal Craft
description: Traditional eco-friendly bamboo craft made using Ringaal bamboo (native Himalayan dwarf bamboo) using intricate weaving patterns passed down through generations.

name: Thulma Blanket
description: Thick, hand-spun and hand-woven woolen blanket featuring a soft, raised furry surface, created traditionally by the Bhotiya weavers of Uttarakhand.
uses: Winter bedding and insulation in extreme cold

name: Bhotiya Dann (Rug)
description: Hand-knotted traditional woolen rug featuring intricate designs with Tibetan and Himalayan motifs, crafted by the Bhotiya tribal artisans.

name: Kumaon Chiura (Chyura) Oil
description: Organic butter/oil extracted from the seeds of the Kumaon butter tree (Diploknema butyracea), traditionally used as a local substitute for ghee.
uses: Natural skin moisturizer and body butter
Raw material for candle and soap making
recipe: Heat Kumaon Chiura oil in a heavy-bottomed pan.
Add semolina and roast on low-medium heat until golden brown and fragrant.
Gently pour in hot water while stirring continuously to avoid lumps.
Add sugar and cardamom powder, mix well, and cook until the halwa thickens and leaves the sides of the pan.
Garnish with chopped nuts and serve hot.
recipe_description: A traditional sweet dish prepared using Chiura (butter tree) oil or butter as a healthy fat base.

name: Copper Products (Tamta Craft)
description: Traditional copper utensils and ritual vessels crafted by the ancient Tamta copper artisans of Almora using hand-beating and engraving methods.
uses: Storing drinking water (known for health benefits)

name: Chaulai (Ramdana)
description: Nutrient-dense Amaranth grain (Ramdana) grown organically on the hill slopes of Uttarakhand. High in protein, fiber, and gluten-free.
ingredients: Cardamom powder, chopped almonds, and pistachios
recipe: Bring milk to a boil in a heavy pan and let it simmer until it reduces slightly.
Add the popped Chaulai seeds to the milk and stir well.
Cook on low heat for 10-12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the mixture thickens.
Stir in the sugar or jaggery and cardamom powder, cooking for another 2 minutes.
Garnish with chopped almonds and pistachios, and serve warm or chilled.
recipe_description: A nutritious and gluten-free dessert made from puffed Ramdana seeds, popular during fasts.

name: Jhangora (Barnyard Millet)
description: Himalayan barnyard millet grown organically in the high-altitude fields. Famous for its low glycemic index, rich mineral content, and digestive benefits.
ingredients: Saffron strands, chopped cashews, and raisins
recipe: Boil the milk in a deep pan, then add the washed Jhangora.
Reduce heat and cook on low flame, stirring constantly to prevent sticking, for 20-25 minutes until the millet is soft.
Add sugar and saffron strands, cooking for another 5 minutes until fully dissolved.
Mix in the cardamom powder and dry fruits. Serve hot or chilled.
recipe_description: A classic Kumaoni dessert made by slow-cooking barnyard millet in milk.

name: Uttarakhand Lal Chawal (Red Rice)
description: Indigenous red-grained rice variety cultivated organically in the Yamuna and Tons river valleys. Famous for its reddish bran layer, high iron content, and nutty taste.
uses: Healthy dietary substitute for white rice
ingredients: Chopped ginger, green chilies, turmeric, salt, 4 cups water
recipe: Wash and soak the Red Rice for at least 1 hour (as it takes longer to cook than white rice).
In a pressure cooker, heat ghee and temper with cumin seeds and Tejpatta.
Add ginger, green chilies, and saute for 1 minute.
Add the soaked Red Rice, washed Moong dal, turmeric, salt, and water.
Close the lid and cook for 4-5 whistles on medium flame. Mash slightly and serve hot with ghee.
recipe_description: A wholesome and healthy mountain comfort food made with red rice and split moong dal.

name: Mandua (Finger Millet)
description: Nutrient-dense finger millet (Ragi) organically grown on mountain slopes. Exceptionally rich in calcium, iron, and fiber.
recipe: Mix Mandua flour, wheat flour, and salt in a bowl.
Gradually add warm water and knead into a soft dough. Let it rest for 10 minutes.
Divide into small balls and roll out gently (use dry wheat flour if sticky).
Place on a hot tawa and cook both sides. Place on open flame to puff up.
Apply fresh ghee generously and serve hot.
recipe_description: Rustic flatbread made from finger millet flour, eaten with ghee or Pahari green vegetables.

name: Almora Lakhori Mirchi
description: Small, highly pungent yellow-colored chilli variety unique to the Lakhori region of Almora. Used as a flavoring agent in local cooking.
ingredients: 1 tbsp lemon juice or tamarind paste, salt to taste
recipe: Dry roast coriander and cumin seeds until fragrant.
Soak Lakhori chilies in warm water for 10 minutes.
Grind the roasted seeds, soaked chilies, garlic, and salt in a mortar and pestle (or blender) into a coarse paste.
Mix in the lemon juice or tamarind paste. Serve with Mandua roti or dal rice.
recipe_description: A spicy and tangy condiment prepared using the fiery Lakhori yellow chili of Almora.

name: Uttarakhand Berinag Tea
description: Premium Orthodox black tea variety harvested from the historic tea gardens of Berinag. Known for its rich aroma, color, and unique Himalayan flavor.
recipe: Boil water in a pan along with crushed ginger and cardamom.
Once boiling, add the Berinag tea leaves and turn off the heat immediately.
Cover with a lid and let it steep for 3-4 minutes to extract the aroma.
Strain the tea into cups and stir in honey to taste.
recipe_description: A soothing tea made from high-quality Berinag tea leaves infused with mountain herbs.

name: Ramnagar Nainital Litchi
description: Juicy, sweet, and highly aromatic litchi fruits grown in the fertile Terai-Bhabhar belt of Ramnagar. Features high pulp content and rich sweetness.
uses: Production of juices, squashes, and preserves
ingredients: 1 cup fresh Ramnagar Litchis (peeled and deseeded)
2 cups chilled water, ice cubes, mint leaves
recipe: Blend the litchi pulp in a blender until smooth. Strain to remove excess fiber.
In a pitcher, combine the litchi juice, lemon juice, sugar syrup/honey, and chilled water.
Stir well and pour into glasses over ice cubes.
Garnish with fresh mint leaves and serve cold.
recipe_description: A refreshing and cooling summer drink made from fresh, sweet Ramnagar litchis.

name: Ramgarh Nainital Aadu (Peach)
description: Juicy and extremely sweet peaches cultivated in the high-altitude orchards of Ramgarh fruit belt, known as the fruit bowl of Kumaon.
ingredients: 4 ripe Ramgarh peaches (peeled, pitted, and sliced)
1 cinnamon stick or pinch of nutmeg
recipe: Combine peach slices, honey, water, and cinnamon in a saucepan.
Bring to a simmer over medium heat.
Reduce heat to low and cook for 10-15 minutes until peaches are soft and the syrup is thick.
Remove the cinnamon stick. Serve warm over pancakes, oatmeal, or vanilla ice cream.
recipe_description: A sweet, warm dessert topping made by simmering fresh Kumaoni peaches with spices.

name: Uttarakhand Malta Fruit
description: Organic citrus fruit rich in Vitamin C, widely grown on the terraced hill slopes. Known for its tangy-sweet flavor.
ingredients: 2 fresh Uttarakhand Maltas (peeled and cut into chunks)
1/2 tsp roasted cumin powder, black salt, and coriander paste
recipe: Place Malta fruit chunks in a bowl.
Add the roasted hemp seed paste, honey, black salt, cumin powder, and coriander paste.
Toss everything gently until the fruit is well coated with the spices.
Enjoy immediately in the warm winter sun.
recipe_description: A traditional winter delicacy made by mixing malta citrus juice with roasted spices and cannabis seeds (bhang).

name: Uttarakhand Pahari Toor Dal
description: Pigeon peas grown organically on mountain slopes. Contains high protein, rich minerals, and cooks into a thick, delicious dal.
uses: Preparation of traditional Pahari dal dishes
ingredients: 1 tsp Jamboo (dry wild chives) or cumin seeds
Turmeric, coriander powder, asafoetida, salt, water
recipe: Wash and boil the Toor Dal in a pressure cooker with water, salt, and turmeric for 3-4 whistles.
Heat ghee in a pan and add a pinch of asafoetida.
Add the dried Jamboo grass (or cumin seeds) and let it crackle and turn aromatic.
Add chopped tomatoes and cook until soft.
Pour the boiled dal into the tempering, mix well, simmer for 5 minutes, and serve with hot rice.
recipe_description: A comforting protein-rich dal cooked with local Himalayan tempering agents like Jamboo.

name: Uttarakhand Gahat (Horse Gram)
description: High-altitude horse gram variety widely known for its medicinal values, particularly for dissolving kidney stones and keeping the body warm in winter.
ingredients: 1 tsp Gandharani (local spice) or cumin seeds
Ginger-garlic paste, coriander powder, turmeric, salt, asafoetida
recipe: Pressure cook the soaked Gahat with water, turmeric, and salt for 6-7 whistles until completely soft.
Heat oil or ghee in a pan, add cumin and asafoetida.
Saute ginger-garlic paste and spices, then pour in the boiled Gahat along with its water.
Simmer on low heat for 15-20 minutes until the dal thickens into a soup-like consistency. Serve hot.
recipe_description: A nutrient-packed horse gram soup/dal famous for its health benefits, particularly in dissolving kidney stones.

name: Uttarakhand Kala Bhat
description: Organic black soybean variety packed with proteins, iron, and antioxidants. A highly revered staple of traditional Kumaoni and Garhwali diets.
uses: Preparation of Bhat ki Churkani (traditional gravy)
ingredients: 2 tbsp wheat flour or rice paste
Ginger-garlic paste, coriander powder, turmeric, red chili, salt
recipe: Heat mustard oil in an iron kadhai. Fry the black soybeans (Kala Bhat) until they crackle and turn aromatic. Remove and keep aside.
In the same oil, add cumin and asafoetida, then saute chopped onions and ginger-garlic paste.
Add wheat flour and roast until brown, then add the spices.
Add the roasted beans and water, bring to a boil, and simmer for 30 minutes on low heat.
Drizzle rice paste or flour slurry to thicken. Cook 5 more minutes and serve with rice.
recipe_description: An iconic Kumaoni curry made from black soybean roasted in mustard oil and thickened with rice paste.

name: Uttarakhand Bichhu Buti (Nettle) Fabric
description: Eco-friendly, highly durable textile woven from the natural bast fibers extracted from the stinging Himalayan nettle plant (Bichhu Ghas).

name: Rangwali Pichhoda (Kumaon)
description: Traditional hand-dyed saffron/yellow Kumaoni dupatta adorned with a central swastika/deity motif and outer red polka dots, representing matrimonial auspiciousness.

name: Chamoli Wooden Ramman Mask
description: Hand-carved wooden masks representing various Hindu deities and characters, used specifically in the UNESCO-recognized Ramman religious ritual theatre of Chamoli.

name: Uttarakhand Likhai (Wood Carving)
description: Exquisite style of traditional architectural wood carving on door frames, window panes, and lintels, featuring rich floral and geometrical motifs.

name: Uttarakhand Buransh Sharbat
description: Refreshing floral syrup concentrate prepared from the crimson petals of the Rhododendron arboreum (Buransh), the state flower of Uttarakhand. Rich in antioxidants.
recipe: In a tall glass, pour the Buransh Sharbat/squash.
Add lemon juice and chilled water or soda.
Stir well to blend the syrup.
Add ice cubes and garnish with mint leaves. Serve chilled.
recipe_description: A sweet and floral beverage made from the syrup of red rhododendron (Buransh) blossoms.

name: Nainital Mombatti (Candle)
description: Famous hand-molded decorative candles with unique colorful layers, floral structures, and traditional designs, produced by the artisans of Nainital.

name: Bal Mithai
description: Traditional sweet of Uttarakhand.
recipe: Cook the Khoya in a heavy pan on low flame, stirring continuously with ghee, until it turns a deep dark brown color (roasted khoya).
Add sugar and mix well until it dissolves and the mixture thickens into a fudge-like consistency.
Pour into a greased tray and let it cool slightly, then cut into rectangular pieces.
Prepare a thin sugar syrup by boiling sugar and water.
Roll the warm khoya pieces in the sugar syrup, then immediately coat them with the white sugar balls.
recipe_description: The iconic dark brown fudge sweet of Almora coated in small white sugar balls.
